coredata.dc:title: Constructing of GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction as efficient piezocatalyst for environmental remediation and understanding the charge transfer mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Ning, Xueer
Hao, Aize
Chen, Ruqi
Khan, Muhammad Farooq
Jia, Dianzeng
coredata.dc:description: Highly efficient catalysts are challenging in the fields of environmental remediation. In this work, for the first time, we combined graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and piezocatalytic materials (ZnO) to construct a novel S-scheme GQDs/ZnO heterojunction via facile method. The obtained GQDs/ZnO heterojunction presented excellent piezocatalytic activity such as higher reaction kinetic rate constant (0.051 min−1) and degradation efficiency (96.1 % within 60 min) as well as superior stability toward methyl orange (MO) dye degradation, far exceeding those of pure ZnO and most reported other typical piezocatalysts. This superior piezocatalytic performance of GQDs/ZnO could be ascribed to S-scheme heterojunction facilitating charge carrier transfer and separation, and enhancing the redox ability as well as the formation atomic-level interfacial bridge of Zn–C–O bond at GQDs/ZnO interface. Furthermore, density functional theory (DFT) calculations demonstrated the charge transfer path of GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction and provided a novel understanding for S-scheme heterojunction mechanism in piezocatalytic field, which supports the experimental evidences of S-scheme heterojunction. This research provides an effective strategy to modulate charge transfer from the atomic level, highlighting an innovative insight for the practical application of highly efficient piezocatalysts in environmental remediation.

head.ce:title: Constructing of GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction as efficient piezocatalyst for environmental remediation and understanding the charge transfer mechanism

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly efficient catalysts are challenging in the fields of environmental remediation. In this work, for the first time, we combined graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and piezocatalytic materials (ZnO) to construct a novel S-scheme GQDs/ZnO heterojunction via facile method. The obtained GQDs/ZnO heterojunction presented excellent piezocatalytic activity such as higher reaction kinetic rate constant (0.051 min−1) and degradation efficiency (96.1 % within 60 min) as well as superior stability toward methyl orange (MO) dye degradation, far exceeding those of pure ZnO and most reported other typical piezocatalysts. This superior piezocatalytic performance of GQDs/ZnO could be ascribed to S-scheme heterojunction facilitating charge carrier transfer and separation, and enhancing the redox ability as well as the formation atomic-level interfacial bridge of Zn–C–O bond at GQDs/ZnO interface. Furthermore, density functional theory (DFT) calculations demonstrated the charge transfer path of GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction and provided a novel understanding for S-scheme heterojunction mechanism in piezocatalytic field, which supports the experimental evidences of S-scheme heterojunction. This research provides an effective strategy to modulate charge transfer from the atomic level, highlighting an innovative insight for the practical application of highly efficient piezocatalysts in environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: In contemporary times, various forms of toxic pollutants (e.g. organic dye, antibiotics and chemicals) in industrial production are being released into water, air, and land, which have detrimental impact on living organisms and environment [1,2]. Consequently, it is crucial to seek for appropriate, environmentally-friendly, and effective methods to deal with these hazardous contaminants. As a response to this need, some innovative catalytic techniques, such as photocatalysis, electrocatalysis and photo-electrocatalysis have been implemented to address the issue of water pollution by degrading organic dyes and antibiotics [3–7]. However, contrasting with these conventional catalytic techniques, the piezocatalytic technique offers unique advantages as it can convert mechanical energy into chemical energy without the need for light or electricity. This is achieved by harnessing natural energy sources such as wind, vibration, water flow, and noise. Consequently, piezocatalysis has garnered significant attention in energy conversion and environmental remediation fields, such as H2 evolution form water splitting, CO2 reduction, H2O2 production, nitrogen fixation and organic pollutants degradation [8–14]. Despite its promising potential, the practical application of piezocatalysis confronts main obstacles in terms of low efficiency, limited charge carrier transfer capability, and poor stability.

As reported, various effective strategies have been proposed to address these mentioned challenges, including morphology regulation, defect engineering, doping engineering, constructing heterostructures, and coupling with other photocatalytic or Fenton reactions etc. [15–20]. Particularly, constructing unique p-n junction, type II and Z-scheme heterojunction via designing suitable materials systems with matching band position, has proven to be an effective approach in promoting piezocatalytic activity [21–24]. More recently, the S-scheme heterojunction has gained significant attentions in the photocatalytic field due to its exceptional redox ability and enhanced charge transfer capacity [25–28]. Nevertheless, the investigation of S-scheme heterojunction catalyst in piezocatalytic area is still less explored. Therefore, there is great interest in exploring the application and mechanism of S-scheme heterojunction to effectively enhance piezocatalytic performance. Besides, it is worth noting that wurtzite ZnO material exhibits good piezoelectric property and has been identified as a favorable candidate catalyst for decontaminating wastewater, nitrogen fixation, and other applications [29–33].

Moreover, the combination of piezocatalytic materials with carbon-based materials probably offer a viable solution to the aforementioned challenges owing to the extraordinary properties of carbon-based materials [34–37]. Among these materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) represent a novel type of zero-dimensional (0D) carbon material with numerous advantages, such as quantum confinement effect, edge effect, good electrochemical performance, and low cost. Additionally, GQDs can serve as electron acceptors, facilitating electron transfer and enhancing charge separation efficiency, which is beneficial for catalytic performance [38,39]. Thus, it appears plausible that the rational design of a GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction would lead to highly efficient piezocatalytic performance in pollutant degradation application. Motivated by these ideas, we have constructed GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction and conducted systematic investigation into the relationship between the charge transfer pathway of GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction and its piezocatalytic performances, as well as the underlying piezocatalytic mechanism.

In this work, we rationally constructed GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction piezocatalyst to effectively boost the degradation performance of organic pollutant under ultrasonic vibration. Interestingly, we observed that S-scheme heterojunction of GQDs/ZnO could enhance redox capacity and Zn–C–O bond was formed to provide atomic-level interfacial bridge, thereby facilitating the transfer and separation of charge carrier for promoting catalytic properties. Moreover, the charge transfer path of GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction was verified through DFT calculations and electron paramagnetic resonance approach. Ultimately, an innovative catalytic mechanism of GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction has been proposed.

ZnO piezocatalyst was prepared using a facile solid-state chemical approach and GQDs was synthesized by hydrothermal process in Supporting Information. GQDs/ZnO heterojunction piezocatalyst was prepared by facile hydrothermal method. Typically, 0.5 g ZnO was dispersed into 50 mL of DI water containing proper amount of GQDs (the weight ratios of GQDs to ZnO was 1 %) with ultrasonication and stirring for 30 min at room temperature to form homogeneous suspension. Afterward, the suspension was transferred into Teflon-lined stainless to maintain at 180 °C for 6 h. After naturally cooled down, the products were collected via filtration by 0.22 μm microporous membrane, washed with water and ethanol for three times, and dried into air for overnight. Finally, the obtained samples were labeled as GQDs/ZnO. Similarly, 0.5 % and 2 % GQDs/ZnO samples were also prepared by changing weight ratios of GQDs to ZnO with 0.5 % and 2 %, respectively.

The crystalline structures, surface chemical states, morphology, piezoelectric property, optical absorption spectra of samples were investigated by XRD, FTIR, XPS, FESEM, HRTEM, PFM and UV-DRS methods in detail in Supporting Information.

The piezocatalytic performances of the GQDs/ZnO were evaluated by degradation organic dye of MO solutions under ultrasonic vibration (frequency of 40 kHz and power of 150 W). In every experiment process, 0.1 g sample was suspended into 100 mL MO dye solutions and magnetically stirred about 30 min under the dark circumstances to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium state before the piezocatalytic activity measurements. Then approximately 3 mL suspension was sampled at the fixed time interval of 5 min and quickly centrifuged to measure through UV–vis absorption spectroscopy with HITACHI U–3900H spectrophotometer. Besides, piezocatalytic reaction kinetic rate constant, degradation efficiency, stability, active species and piezo-electrochemical performances of samples were assessed in detail in Supporting Information.

All calculations were conducted by Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP) with the frozen-core all-electron projector-augment-wave (PAW) method [40–42]. Exchange and correlation energies for all systems were treated under generalized gradient approximation (GGA) proposed through Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) [43]. The cut-off energy of plane wave was set to 450 eV. To include dispersion interactions, Grimme's DFT-D3 method was adopted [44]. Convergence criteria for electronic and ionic relaxations were set to 10−5 eV and 0.02 eV/Å, respectively. Dipole correction along Z-direction was included in the calculations. Brillouin zone was sampled with Γ-centered (1 × 1 × 1) k meshes. A p(5 × 3) ZnO (100) supercell with three layers was employed, the bottom two layers were frozen to their bulk positions, and remaining one layer was relaxed. Vacuum thickness was set to 16 to avoid interaction between two adjacent layers.

Crystalline structures of obtained piezocatalysts were investigated and confirmed through XRD analysis. As displayed in Fig. 1 (a), all diffraction peaks of ZnO were observed at 31.7°, 34.3°, 36.2°, 47.5°, 56.5°, 62.8°, 66.3°, 67.9°, 69.0°, 76.9°, which are accurately assigned to the crystal planes (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), (201), (202), well indexed to the hexagonal phase of ZnO (JCPDS No. 36-1451) [19,31]. Notably, the diffraction peaks of GQDs/ZnO are similar to that of ZnO, with no observable diffraction peak of GQDs. This can be ascribed to the trace amount and poor crystallinity of GQDs [44,45].

To further validate the presence of GQDs and the formation of GQDs/ZnO, Raman technology was recorded. The results (Fig. 1(b) and Fig. S1(a)) depict characteristic peaks of ZnO located at 380, 408, 438 and 584 cm−1, corresponding to A1(TO), E1(TO), E2 high and E1(LO) of the first-order optical phonon modes. Due to the multiple-phonon scattering processes, the peaks at 332, 536, 1072 and 1158 cm−1 can be attributed to the E2 high-E2 low, 2B1 low, TO + LO and 2LO, respectively [46,47]. After introduction of GQDs, the Raman spectra of GQDs/ZnO obviously exhibit the typical characteristic GQDs peaks at 1342 (D-band) and 1580 cm−1 (G-band), which correspond to the disorder and planner vibration of sp2 bonded carbon structure, respectively [46]. Significantly, the red shift of the E2 high peak (at 430 cm−1) evidently supports the successful formation of the GQDs/ZnO heterojunction, suggesting strong interactions between the GQDs and ZnO.

Furthermore, the XPS technology was utilized to determine the composition, chemical states and interfacial interaction of GQDs/ZnO piezocatalyst. Fig. 1(c) exhibits the survey spectra of ZnO and GQDs/ZnO, revealing the presence of Zn 2p, O 1s and C 1s. As to the GQDs/ZnO, high-resolution XPS spectrogram (Fig. 1(d)) of Zn 2p shows two peaks at 1021.5 and 1044.6 eV are belong to the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 states, respectively. In O 1s XPS spectra of GQDs/ZnO (Fig. 1(e)), the asymmetric nature peaks at 530.1 and 531.7 eV stemmed from lattice O2− ions for Zn–O bonding and oxygen in form of OH group on the surface of ZnO, respectively [19]. In comparison to pure ZnO, binding energy of Zn 2p and O 1s in GQDs/ZnO are significantly shifted to lower energy levels with the increase of electron density, revealing the strong interfacial interaction between ZnO and GQDs. This observation confirms the formation of GQDs/ZnO heterojunction, where the electron transfer occurs from GQDs to surface of ZnO. In addition, Fig. 1(f) illustrates that apart from the characteristic peaks of CC and C–O groups, several characteristic peaks of CO and Zn–C–O occur, providing further evidence for the presence of GQDs. Notably, Zn–C–O bond is observed from GQDs/ZnO, which implies the strong chemical interactions in GQDs/ZnO interface, endowing faster electron transfer and maintaining structural stability. These findings are in agreement with the results obtained from FT-IR spectroscopy, as proved in Fig. S1(b), which displays stretching vibrations of C–O at 1203 cm−1, CC at 1620 cm−1, and CO at 1720 cm−1 [48]. As proved by these results, it can be concluded that the formation of GQDs/ZnO heterojunction induces a strong interfacial interaction between ZnO and GQDs, which is likely beneficial for optimizing the pathway for charge carrier transfer and enhancing the intrinsic activity of piezocatalyst.

Moreover, details morphology features of GQDs, ZnO and GQDs/ZnO were investigated through SEM and TEM measurements. In Fig. 2 (a–c), the typical TEM images clearly depict a uniform distribution of GQDs, with average grain size of 5.08 nm and lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. The SEM and TEM images of ZnO (Fig. 2(d–f)) present a relatively flat and uniform nanocrystalline morphology, with an average diameter of approximately 40 nm. The observed 0.28 nm lattice spacing can be attributed to the growth direction of the hexagonal wurtzite ZnO (100) plane. Fig. 2(g–i) exhibit the morphology of GQDs/ZnO, with the HRTEM image in Fig. 2(i) confirming the formation of GQDs/ZnO heterojunction. Additionally, the orderly distribution of GQDs in GQDs/ZnO was verified through energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), as shown in Fig. 2(j–m), where the overall distribution of C element confirms that GQDs are homogeneously loaded on the surface of ZnO nanocrystal.

It is widely recognized that the charge transfer and piezoelectric response of piezocatalytic materials play a crucial role in regulating catalytic activity under mechanical stress excitation. Piezoelectric response force microscopy (PFM) technology was employed to provide direct proof of the piezoelectric property and strength of the piezoelectric potential of ZnO and GQDs/ZnO. From the representative amplitude and phase diagrams of GQDs/ZnO as shown in Fig. 3 (a–b), the images exhibit distinct contrast variations and the piezoelectric potential signal was calculated to be 27.6 mV, suggesting that GQDs/ZnO has a high piezoelectric potential and robust piezoelectricity compared to other typical piezocatalysts [49,50]. Besides, a well-matched phase and amplitude maps verify the piezoelectric performance of ZnO and GQDs/ZnO. According to the butterfly amplitude-voltage loops as illustrate from Fig. 3(c) and Fig. S2(a), the piezoelectric constants (d 33) were calculated by Equation (1) [51]:
(1)
d 33=(A-A
0 )/(V–V
0 )
where V and A denote applied voltage and amplitude, respectively. V
0 and A
0 are the intersection point of amplitude butterfly loop curve. GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction has larger d 33 values (39.8 pm V−1) than that of pure ZnO (14.9 pm V−1). Furthermore, the well-established phase hysteresis loops of GQDs/ZnO and ZnO are observed in Fig. 3(d) and Fig. S2(b). Phase angle presents near ∼180° local switching changes with sweeping applying a tip bias of ±10 V, proving the occurrence of polarization reversal process. The difference in the phase-voltage curve between ZnO and GQDs/ZnO is mainly ascribed to the weakness piezoelectricity of the ZnO. It will be an advantageous support for further confirming the good piezoelectric response of GQDs/ZnO and imply the strong non-zero remnant polarization, favorable to further enhance piezocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of piezocatalytic degradation activity and stability
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the potential application of GQDs/ZnO for degradation of organic pollutant, MO was employed as a model pollutant, and the piezocatalytic degradation capability and corresponding reaction kinetics of all samples were systematically analyzed. Fig. 4 (a) depicts the piezocatalytic degradation curves of different samples. It is noteworthy that MO dye can slightly be degraded with absence of catalyst, whereas GQDs/ZnO exhibits prominently enhanced catalytic activity compared to ZnO under the same ultrasonic vibration condition. In Fig. S3(a), intensity of absorption peak at approximately 463 nm declined sharply with extension of ultrasonic vibration time in the presence of GQDs/ZnO, then almost disappears within 60 min, proving the complete degradation of MO by the utilization of GQDs/ZnO. Obviously, the process of piezocatalytic degradation follows first-order kinetics (Fig. S3(b)) and the corresponding k values in Fig. 4(b), k value of GQDs/ZnO is 51.05 × 10−3 min−1, which is approximately 7-fold higher than that of pure ZnO (k = 7.60 × 10−3 min−1). This value ranks higher than that of reported most of ZnO and others typical piezocatalysts in Fig. 4(c) and Table S1 [19,30,31,52–55]. Fig. 4(d) displays that degradation efficiency of GQDs/ZnO reaches 96.1 % within 60 min, which is much higher than pristine ZnO (38.0 %). This result further verifies the remarkable improved piezocatalytic activity of GQDs/ZnO, highlighting its crucial role in practical application for wastewater purification. In practical application, the stability of the catalyst is a significant indicator for assessing the cost-effectiveness of the process on a larger scale. In order to evaluate the piezocatalytic stability of GQDs/ZnO, the cyclic test experiment was conducted. Fig. 4(e) exhibits that degradation efficiency of GQDs/ZnO still remains at 91.3 % even after four consecutive cycles tests, demonstrating its exceptional cyclic stability. Furthermore, the XRD and SEM images (Fig. S4(a-c)) indicate that the structure and morphology of GQDs/ZnO are well-preserved following the cyclic tests, providing further confirmation of its superior stability.

Generally, various influencing factors (e.g. catalyst dosage, solution pH) play a significant role in advanced oxidation processes. Therefore, the influence of experimental factors including GQDs ratio, catalyst dosage, solution pH and ultrasonic vibration power on the decomposition of MO dye was further investigated. The results are presented in Figs. S5(a–d). It can be observed from Fig. S5(a) that the MO degradation is affected by different ratio of GQDs/ZnO catalyst from 0 to 2 %. The MO degradation efficiency is significantly improved as the GQDs ratio from 0 to 1 %. Nevertheless, the efficiency sharply drops when the GQDs ratio reaches 2 %, indicating that an excessive amount of GQDs inhibits the MO decomposition process. Thus, the optimal GQDs ratio is selected as 1 % for all subsequent MO decomposition experiments. Furthermore, Fig. S5(b) demonstrates that with a catalyst dosage of 100 mg of GQDs/ZnO, the decomposition efficiency substantially increases and achieves nearly complete degradation of MO within 60 min, compared to a 50 mg catalyst dosage. These results suggest that an increased catalyst dosage is favorable up to a certain extent. However, there is little difference observed in MO removal with a higher catalyst dosage of 150 mg. Consequently, a GQDs/ZnO catalyst dosage of 100 mg is chosen as the optimum dosage, minimizing the input of catalyst while achieving highly efficient degradation rates in practical applications. Additionally, the solution pH probably has a considerable impact on advanced oxidation processes reactions as it affects the formation of active radicals and catalytic reaction kinetics. As shown in Fig. S5(c), the MO degradation efficiency for GQDs/ZnO remains relatively high, above 72 %, across a wide range of pH values (3–9). It is worth noting that a pH value of 7 is selected as the optimum value, and subsequent experiments were conducted based on this value due to its highest efficiency. Furthermore, the effect of ultrasonic power on the piezocatalytic degradation of GQDs/ZnO is also examined (Fig. S5(d)). It is evident that the MO degradation efficiency increases with higher ultrasonic vibration power (0, 50, 100 and 150 W). This acceleration is attributed to the increase in strain and enhanced piezoelectric polarization, triggering more efficient separation and migration of charge carriers [8]. To further validate the potential application of GQDs/ZnO catalyst, MO degradation experiment was also conducted under wastewater condition (Fig. S6). Based on the differences in water quality, a reduced degradation efficiency in wastewater was observed after 60 min. This reduction is mainly attributed to the presence of other inorganic ions that hinder the degradation reactions [9,56]. Therefore, the development of GQDs/ZnO catalyst holds important scientific significance and application value in the treatment of organic dye pollutants in practical environmental remediation field.

With the aim of providing deeper understanding of piezocatalytic degradation behavior. The energy-band structures, electronic structure, electron-transfer ability and interfacial charge properties of ZnO, GQDs and GQDs/ZnO were calculated in the framework of first-principles density functional theory method. Figs. S7(a–f) exhibits the top and side views of the crystal structure models for ZnO, GQDs and GQDs/ZnO. A p(5 × 3) ZnO (100) supercell with three layers was simulated and used for subsequent calculation. Due to the ultra-fine size, GQDs can be performed as a structural unit composed of six connected benzene rings [44]. The geometrically optimized structure of GQDs/ZnO heterojunction in Fig. S7(f), it can clearly observe that the bridge sites of recombination modes were formed for their relatively stable [57]. In the GQDs/ZnO model, the carbon atom closest to the origin of coordinate is above the bonding between Zn atom and O atom, forming the bonding of Zn–C–O.

The energy-band structure of ZnO was displayed in Fig. 5 (a). Conduction band (CB) minimum and the valence band (VB) maximum of ZnO (100) are high-symmetry located at the G point, with an energy of 3.0 eV, thus further confirming that ZnO is direct band-gap semiconductors. However, because of the limitation of DFT method, the calculated band-gap value of ZnO is slight smaller than the experimental result as previously reports [19,58,59].

As exhibited in Fig. 5(b–c), the calculated density of states (DOS) on ZnO and GQDs/ZnO indicates that the valence bands of the investigated materials are mainly consists of the Zn 3d and O 2p orbitals. Moreover, the hybridization between Zn 3d-orbital and O 2p-orbital are greatly enhanced around the Fermi level due to contribution of GQDs, revealing a favorable charge transfer process with bridge sites bonding of Zn–C–O for GQDs/ZnO heterojunction. Additionally, the total electron density (TDOS) differences of ZnO and GQDs/ZnO were calculated to reveal the electrons redistribution. Compared with original ZnO, after introduction of GQDs, the surrounding electrons are attracted to Zn and O atoms, exhibiting rich-electron local environment. The increasing local charge density around the Zn and O atoms are beneficial to restrict the recombination of electron and hole. Regarding the overall electron distribution, the electron density of GQDs/ZnO is dramatically stronger with more than an order of magnitude higher compared to that of pure ZnO. This implies the introduction of GQDs can induce high electrical conductivity and good electron mobility as well as produce more electron to accelerate charge carrier transfer during catalytic reaction [38,44]. To explore the electron-transfer pathway and physicochemical property, the average potential profiles along c-axis direction to determine work functions (Φ) values of three materials are exhibited in Fig. 5(d–f). The blue and red dotted lines indicate the position of the Vacuum level (Evac) and Fermi level (Ef). Our results show that the Φ values of GQDs, ZnO and GQDs/ZnO are 5.24, 5.28 and 4.99 eV, respectively. Upon contact between GQDs and ZnO, driven by the difference in Fermi level, the electron can transfer from the higher Fermi level of GQDs to the lower Fermi level of ZnO until equilibrium is reached, thus forming an internal electric field at interface between GQDs and ZnO. Under the synergistic effect of the built-in electric field and piezoelectric potential, the electron transport behavior is optimized, the effective separation of charge carriers is accelerated, and their recombination is prevented, further facilitating the piezoelectric catalytic degradation activity. Notably, GQDs/ZnO exhibits the smaller work function in comparison to that of original ZnO, suggesting that GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction is favorable charge transfer in catalytic process. Moreover, the calculated electron density distribution was explored to elucidate the charge transfer behavior in Fig. 5(g–h). The different perspectives of top and side views clearly illustrated the charge can transfer from the C atom to the attached Zn and O atom, which is evidenced by the blue and yellow areas with lower and higher electron density, respectively. Quantitatively, the Bader charge analysis indicates that the C atom of GQDs obtains about 0.95e from the surrounding ZnO, the corresponding atomic numbers are shown in Fig. 5(i) and the values of bader charge distribution are presented in the form of Table S2.

Furthermore, the bonding status and electronic transmission of Zn–C–O bridge sites heterojunction was carried out by two-dimensional (2D) differential charge density, providing valuable insight into the charge transfer mechanism. The three-dimensional (3D) crystal structure of the bridge sites heterojunction (see Fig. S8(a)) was utilized to better visualize the interactions between the target atoms, in which purple cross section represents the section of 2D differential charge density. As illustrated in Fig. S8(b), the coplanar 2D charge density difference diagram of C, O and Zn atoms consist of two carbon atoms, one oxygen atom and one zinc atom, with the blue area as the region of charge loss and the red area as the region of charge gain. A significant amount of electron transfer behaver occurs between the Zn, O and C atoms, the specific electron gain and loss can be referred to the color scale on the right of Fig. S8(b). Obviously, after the GQDs and ZnO (100) to form the S-scheme heterojunction, the carbon atoms of GQDs loses electrons from the Zn and O atoms surface below, thus by enhance the electron transfer capability, adjusting the charge carrier concentration of the material. Based on these theoretical calculation results, it is reasonable to expect that such the S-scheme heterojunction probably result in the effective separation of charge carrier as well as the reduction of work function, which is conductive to the improvement of piezocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of optical property and energy band diagram
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the energy band structure of GQDs/ZnO, a series of UV–vis diffuse reflectance (UV–Vis DRS) and XPS valence band (XPS-VB) techniques were performed. As depicted in Fig. 6 (a), the absorption edge of pure ZnO is observed at around 400 nm, indicating its wide band gap feature, with negligible absorption of visible light. Especially, Fig. 6(b) shows that the optical absorption spectra of GQDs suspensions display a strong absorption peak located at around 248 nm, corresponding to the π→π* transition in the sp2 domains, a characteristic feature of graphene-based materials [60]. Additionally, the spectra of the GQDs display a distinct absorption shoulder at around 360 nm, which can be ascribed to an electron transition from the n-states to the π* orbital in the molecular orbital of carbonyl groups (CO) in GQDs [52]. It is worth noting that the GQDs solution appeared yellowish liquid under sunlight but emitted bright blue light under UV light (λ = 354 nm) irradiation, as seen in the inset. These findings explicitly confirm that the optical properties and corresponding average grain size of as-prepared GQDs, in accordance with previously reported values [38]. Furthermore, the Plots of (αhν)2 versus (hν) was adopted to assess optical band gaps of ZnO and GQDs, which are calculated to be 3.16 and 2.96 eV as exhibited in Fig. 6(a) inset and Fig. 6(c). The corresponding calculation process involves Kubelka-Munk function: αhν = A(hν-Eg)1/2 through linear extrapolation approach [19,53]. To comprehensively analyze the energy band structure of samples, we carried out the XPS-VB experiments (Fig. 6(d–e)) and estimated valence band position for ZnO and GQDs to be 2.76 and 1.29 eV. Using the formula: EVB-NHE = Ф + 2.76 eV–4.4 and EVB-NHE = Ф + 1.29 eV–4.4, respectively (EVB-NHE: the VB potential vs. standard hydrogen electrode potential, Ф of 4.50 eV: electron work function of analyzer), the EVB-NHE values of ZnO and GQDs are 2.86 and 1.39 V, respectively. According to Nernst formula [53,61]: ECB = EVB – Eg, the conduction band potential (ECB) edge positions of ZnO and GQDs are determined −0.30 and −1.57 V. According to aforementioned analysis, the energy band diagram of GQDs/ZnO is clearly illustrated in Fig. 6(f). More exactly, the ECB and EVB positions of ZnO and GQDs are well matched to form S-scheme heterojunction, which benefits highly efficient charge carrier separation and transfer to generate more active free radicals during the degradation process. In addition, the energy band positions of GQDs/ZnO are well matched with chemical redox potential, implying the good feasibility during catalytic degradation process on the basis of thermodynamics principle [25,26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of charge transfer and piezo-electrochemical performances
ce:section.ce:para: Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) are a promising approach to address wastewater pollution, which has been proven to be highly effective in degrading organic pollutants via producing strong oxidizing active species such as hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and superoxide radicals (·O2 −) [9]. In order to gain further insight into the impact of free radicals during piezocatalytic oxidation, a series of comparative tests were conducted, in which various additives were poured into the reaction system, as depicted in Fig. 7 (a). When IPA and BQ were added to the GQDs/ZnO reaction system, a significant decrease in the decomposition rate of MO dye is observed. Conversely, the addition of EDTA and AgNO3 only yield a slight decrease in the degradation ratio of MO dye. It is important to note that the inhibitory effects of the four scavengers followed the order of IPA > BQ > EDTA > AgNO3. The corresponding rate constants (k values) are found to be 0.01013, 0.01574, 0.03246 and 0.03691 min−1, respectively, as shown in Fig. 7(b). These results indicate that the GQDs/ZnO piezocatalyst can generate abundant ·OH and ·O2 − radicals, thereby facilitating the degradation of organic pollutants in advanced oxidation processes.

To better to further confirm the S-scheme heterojunction mechanism of GQDs/ZnO, the EPR technology was conducted. In Fig. 7(c–d), the high signals of DMPO−·O2 − and DMPO−·OH of GQDs/ZnO were obviously detected after 30 min of ultrasonic vibration, displaying that GQDs/ZnO can induce effective concentration of strong redox radicals. In addition, the EPR signals intensity difference of ZnO, GQDs and GQDs/ZnO samples can indirectly validate the piezocatalytic generated charge transfer and S-scheme heterojunction mechanism of GQDs/ZnO. The DMPO-·O2 − intensity of GQDs/ZnO is clearly higher than pristine GQDs. But DMPO-·O2 − signal of ZnO is scarcely observed. This is attributed to the reduction reaction potential CB of GQDs (−1.57 V) is negative than the redox potential of O2/·O2 − couples (−0.33 V). Similarly, DMPO-·OH signal of GQDs/ZnO is evidently found stronger than ZnO because that the oxidation reaction potential VB of ZnO (2.86 V) is positive than the redox potential of OH−/·OH (1.99 V) couples. The above results provide strong evidence that the charge transfer mechanism of GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction, preservation the higher reduction and oxidation ability and formation of a large number of ·OH and ·O2 − free radicals, which is conductive to enhancing the catalytic decomposition activity. Additionally, Fig. 7(e–f) illustrates that the fluorescence intensity of 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (as ·OH capture agent) significantly increases with longer vibration times, indicating the production of ·OH of ZnO and GQDs/ZnO under vibration condition. Notably, GQDs/ZnO exhibits stronger fluorescence intensity than pure ZnO, suggesting that GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction can harness vibration energy to generate more piezoelectric charge, which in turn promote the production of ·OH, thereby enhancing the piezocatalytic activity. These findings provide valuable insights into the piezocatalytic mechanism of S-scheme heterojunction.

As shown in Fig. 8 (a), the steady-state photoluminescence spectrum of GQDs/ZnO is significantly quenched and attenuated compared with pure ZnO, suggesting that the recombination of charge carriers is greatly inhibited in GQDs/ZnO [62]. Furthermore, transient current response measurement was employed to reveal the charge carrier separation and mobility ability. Curves of transient current density versus ultrasound time with several On and Off cycles of ZnO and GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction is exhibited in Fig. 8(b). Current density of GQDs/ZnO is clearly enhanced and shows apparent steps between On and Off state than ZnO, indicating that the charge carrier separation capability of GQDs/ZnO is far exceeded than original ZnO. Moreover, EIS Nyquist plots of ZnO and GQDs/ZnO were conducted by ultrasonic vibration in Fig. 8(c). The EIS Nyquist plots arc radius of GQDs/ZnO is remarkable smaller than ZnO, implying lower transfer resistance of charge carrier. Thus, we demonstrate that GQDs/ZnO can induce eﬀicient charge carrier separation and accelerate interfacial charge transfer, facilitating charge carrier transport for promoting piezocatalytic performance.

Overall, a novel insight into the S-scheme heterojunction piezocatalytic mechanism of the GQDs/ZnO has been put forward and clarified in Fig. 9 (a–c) based on our experimental and theoretical calculations results. Fig. 9(a) presents that GQDs has a higher Fermi level than that of ZnO before contact. When the direct contact between GQDs and ZnO occurs, the atomic-level interfacial bridge of Zn–C–O bond at interface is formed and the free electrons will spontaneously migrate from the GQDs to the ZnO. Consequently, internal electric filed is also formed at the interface owing to the GQDs loses electrons and ZnO receives electrons. Meanwhile, the band edge of GQDs bends upward because of electron depletion, whereas that of ZnO bends downwards owing to electron accumulation as shown in Fig. 9(b) [26,46,63]. When the GQDs/ZnO is under strain (Fig. 9(c)), the band position of ZnO is easily tilted and a certain number of positive and negative polarization charges (q+ and q−) on ZnO surface are generated owing to the piezoelectric effect based on piezocatalytic theory [19,64]. As a result, a built-in electric field is established and high piezoelectric potential (Pz) will generate.

Besides, the low concentration of intrinsic free charge carrier by thermally or mechanically activation are also generated [9,14,65]. According to the GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction, the weak reducibility electrons in the CB of ZnO can easily recombine with weak oxidation holes in the VB of GQDs, which is favorable to accelerate the electric charge carrier separation while maintaining strong redox potential. Simultaneously, strong oxidation holes on the VB of ZnO will react with OH − to generate ·OH radical, remarkable increasing the concentration of reactive species in catalytic decomposition of organic contamination process. Besides, strong reducibility electrons on the CB of GQDs will react with the surface absorbing O2 to generate ·O2 − radical. Consequently, a large number of strong ·OH and ·O2 − active reactive radicals in advanced oxidation processes can be induced by built-in electric field and piezoelectric potential synergistic effect for effective degradation of MO dye to harmless products (e.g. CO2 and H2O) [19,25,61,65]. A series of reactive reactions in GQDs/ZnO piezocatalytic decomposition process are elucidated as following Equations (2)–(6):
(2)
GQDs/ZnO + Strain → GQDs/ZnO (e
- + h
+
)
(3)
ZnO (e
-
) + GQDs (h

) → Recombination
(4)

(6)

O (Harmless products)

Our results confirm that the excellent piezocatalytic decomposition performances of GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction is basically ascribed to superior redox ability as well as modulation of charge carrier transport via strong built-in electric field. This work offers an innovative piezocatalytic mechanism and provides scientific evidence to support the rational design and application of highly efficient ZnO-based piezocatalysts in environmental sewage disposal.

To conclude, we have successfully designed and constructed a novel GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction, which has been applied to the field of piezocatalysis. As supported by both related experimental analyses and density functional theory calculations, S-scheme charge transfer pathway and piezocatalytic mechanism of GQDs/ZnO has been unveiled. This mechanism involves facilitating charge transfer, improving the redox ability, and providing atomic-level interfacial bridge through a chemically bonded approach. As a result, the GQDs/ZnO S-scheme heterojunction has demonstrated excellent piezocatalytic activity and superior stability in recycling experiments, surpassing numerous previously reported piezocatalytic materials systems. This strategy holds great potential for stimulating interest in diverse piezocatalytic applications, extending beyond environmental remediation to include H2 evolution, CO2 reduction, N2 piezo-fixation and biological anti-bacterial in industrial production.
